coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-Zno/TiO2 ternary nanocomposite as a proficient material to enhance the performance of natural DSSC
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Varghese, Meera
Ninan, Gisa Grace
Balachandran, Manoj
coredata.dc:description: A novel sustainable approach for enhancing the efficiency of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) involves the utilization of a combination of ZnO and carbon dots (CDs) derived from Citrus medica fruit extract, along with microwave-synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles for the preparation of the photoanode. Natural dyes such as Hibiscus rosa-sinensis and Allium Cepa peel are employed as sensitizers to reduce production costs. This co-activation method has demonstrated a significant improvement in the output parameters of the devices. Notably, the photoanode co-activated with ZnO-CD composite (ZnO-CD/TiO2) exhibits the most favorable output parameters when combined with Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye (open circuit voltage (Voc) = 0.80 V, short circuit current density (Jsc) = 6.62 mA/cm2, fill factor (FF) = 64.20 %, photo conversion efficiency (PCE) = 3.40 %) and Allium Cepa peel dye (Voc = 0.81 V, Jsc = 6.79 mA/cm2, FF = 65.70 %, PCE = 3.61 %). When paired with Allium Cepa dye, the CD modified photoanode (CD/TiO2) offers Voc = 0.73 V, Jsc = 6.64 mA/cm2, FF = 61.27 % and PCE = 2.97 %. Similarly, when combined with Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye, the output parameters of the CD/TiO2 photoanode are Voc = 0.72 V, Jsc = 6.54 mA/cm2, FF = 64.4 % and PCE = 3.03 %. In comparison to all tested devices, the unmodified photoanode (TiO2) displayed the lowest performance, with parameters such as Voc = 0.59 V, Jsc = 6.45 mA/cm2, FF = 52.5 %, PCE = 2.10 % using Allium Cepa peel dye, and Voc = 0.66 V, Jsc = 6 mA/cm2, FF = 51.60 %, PCE = 2.04 % using Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye. Furthermore, the co-activation process has been shown to enhance the stability of the devices. While the unmodified photoanodes ceased to operate after eight days, the ZnO-CD composite co-activated photoanodes retained their initial efficiencies up to 61.50 % and 68.53 % with the Allium Cepa peel dye and Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye, respectively. Therefore, this study underscores the potential of the synthesized composite material in enhancing the performance of natural DSSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DSSC
Natural dye
Carbon dots
Composites
Co-activation
Stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs and ZnO-CDs nanocomposite
Hibiscus rosa-sinensis and Allium Cepa peel dye extraction
Structural and morphological analysis of ZnO-CDs/TiO2
Performance and stability analysis of fabricated DSSCs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Centre for Research and Central Instrumentation Facility

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Centre for Research and Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF), CHRIST (Deemed to be University) for their generous financial support under file no. SMSS-2221 and for providing the necessary instrumentation facilities that were instrumental in the successful completion of this research. Furthermore, the authors would like to express their gratitude to DST-FIST for providing the research fund under SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C). The authors would also like to acknowledge the kind support of SAIF-STIC, CUSAT (Kerala) and SAIF-MGU (Kerala) in the characterization studies that were conducted for this research. Their assistance was invaluable in enabling the completion of our study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs and ZnO-CDs nanocomposite.
a) XRD profiles of CDs, TiO2, ZnO, and ZnO-CDs/TiO2, b) TEM image of ZnO-CDs/TiO2 composite, c) HR-TEM image of the ZnO-CDs/TiO2 composite with Fourier transform (FFT) images and lattice spacings.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) bandgap calculation of TiO2, CDs/TiO2 and ZnO-CDs/TiO2.
(a–c) Comparison of the J-V parameters of the fabricated devices sensitized with Allium Cepa peel dye and (d–f) Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye studied up to 60 days.

The J-V graph of DSSC using ZnO-CDs/TiO2 photoanode sensitized with (a) Allium Cepa dye and (b) Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye tested up to 60 days. (c) Efficiencies of DSSC devices with TiO2, CDs/TiO2 and ZnO-CDs photoanodes sensitized with Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye and Allium Cepa peel dye studied up to 60 days in an interval of 10 days. (d) Percentage efficiency improvement of the co-activated devices calculated with reference to unmodified photoanode and the percentage retained initial efficiency after 60 days.
Recent literature highlights stability issues associated with natural dye-sensitized solar cells.
Comparison table of output parameters for co-activated DSSC devices reported in the literature using various natural dyes.

entry.italic: Terminalia catappa Azadirachta indica Clitoria ternatea

row.entry:
Achiote seeds
Bark of Brazil
Bark of Taray

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-Zno/TiO2 ternary nanocomposite as a proficient material to enhance the performance of natural DSSC

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ University, Bengaluru, Karnataka, 560029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Electronics, Christ University, Bengaluru, Karnataka, 560029, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel sustainable approach for enhancing the efficiency of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) involves the utilization of a combination of ZnO and carbon dots (CDs) derived from Citrus medica fruit extract, along with microwave-synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles for the preparation of the photoanode. Natural dyes such as Hibiscus rosa-sinensis and Allium Cepa peel are employed as sensitizers to reduce production costs. This co-activation method has demonstrated a significant improvement in the output parameters of the devices. Notably, the photoanode co-activated with ZnO-CD composite (ZnO-CD/TiO2) exhibits the most favorable output parameters when combined with Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye (open circuit voltage (Voc) = 0.80 V, short circuit current density (Jsc) = 6.62 mA/cm2, fill factor (FF) = 64.20 %, photo conversion efficiency (PCE) = 3.40 %) and Allium Cepa peel dye (Voc = 0.81 V, Jsc = 6.79 mA/cm2, FF = 65.70 %, PCE = 3.61 %). When paired with Allium Cepa dye, the CD modified photoanode (CD/TiO2) offers Voc = 0.73 V, Jsc = 6.64 mA/cm2, FF = 61.27 % and PCE = 2.97 %. Similarly, when combined with Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye, the output parameters of the CD/TiO2 photoanode are Voc = 0.72 V, Jsc = 6.54 mA/cm2, FF = 64.4 % and PCE = 3.03 %. In comparison to all tested devices, the unmodified photoanode (TiO2) displayed the lowest performance, with parameters such as Voc = 0.59 V, Jsc = 6.45 mA/cm2, FF = 52.5 %, PCE = 2.10 % using Allium Cepa peel dye, and Voc = 0.66 V, Jsc = 6 mA/cm2, FF = 51.60 %, PCE = 2.04 % using Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye. Furthermore, the co-activation process has been shown to enhance the stability of the devices. While the unmodified photoanodes ceased to operate after eight days, the ZnO-CD composite co-activated photoanodes retained their initial efficiencies up to 61.50 % and 68.53 % with the Allium Cepa peel dye and Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye, respectively. Therefore, this study underscores the potential of the synthesized composite material in enhancing the performance of natural DSSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The pursuit of renewable energy is a fundamental aspect of establishing a sustainable future and solar energy represents one of the most promising sources [1]. DSSCs constitute the third generation of solar cell technology and present a viable alternative to traditional silicon-based photovoltaic cells. It offers a range of benefits, including cost-effectiveness, high photo conversion efficiency (PCE), eco-friendliness, and straightforward manufacturing processes [2]. Moreover, DSSCs perform well in indoor environments and are less sensitive to the angle of sunlight, which provides a significant advantage [3,4]. Additionally, they are relatively inexpensive to produce and require simple fabrication processes. Nevertheless, DSSCs require further improvements in efficiency and stability compared to traditional solar cells [5,6]. Several methods have been attempted to modify the conventional TiO2 photoanode, which significantly impacts the efficiency of DSSC [7–9].

To realize the full potential of TiO2 for solar cell applications, it is crucial to integrate a highly active surface area photoanode with a favorable morphology and superior light-scattering effect. Composite photoanodes are being explored to benefit from the large specific surface area, light-scattering effect, reduced recombination and promoted electron transfer rate [10]. In a recent report, Ag–Au@TiO2 nanocomposites were identified as an efficient photoanode due to their enhanced light harvesting properties, which result from improved electron transfer ability onto TiO2 [11]. Ni-doped TiO2/TiO2 nanocomposites were employed to achieve a higher short-circuit current density, leading to an enhanced efficiency of DSSCs [12]. ZnO nanoparticles and TiO2 share similar characteristics with wide bandgap properties. However, ZnO outperforms TiO2 in charge transport and overall effectiveness in DSSC cells due to its higher electron mobility. Nevertheless, pure ZnO has some limitations, such as suboptimal sensitizer adsorption, accelerated electron recombination, and a heightened risk of sensitizer degradation [13,14]. Therefore, extensive research has been conducted on the properties of nanostructured ZnO–TiO2 composites, which have exhibited impressive photo conversion capabilities compared to their individual constituents. These composites are characterized by superior charge transport, an extended lifespan for hole-electron pairs, and an augmented open circuit voltage [15,16].

The long-term stability of DSSC is a major challenge as they are prone to efficiency degradation over time due to photodegradation from exposure to sunlight. Various researchers have tested the stability of DSSC devices sensitized with natural dyes (Table 1 ). For instance, Ammar et al. fabricated DSSC using Chlorophyll from spinach leaves and Anthocyanin from red cabbage and onion peels as photosensitizers. The device utilizing Chlorophyll demonstrated superior performance, with an initial efficiency of 0.17 %, which decreased to 0.08 % after seven days [1]. In a separate study, Najihah et al. utilized Costus woodsonii leaf extract as a natural sensitizer for DSSC, resulting in a device with an initial efficiency of 0.65 %, which decreased to 0.41 % after 96 h [2]. Papaya leaves-based DSSCs, developed by Arifin et al., were subjected to stability testing 200 h after fabrication. Among the dyes, Fe-Chlorophyllin showed higher efficiency, while crude Chlorophyll exhibited better stability, retaining 98.6 % of the initial efficiency [3]. Furthermore, a curcumin-based natural dye-sensitized solar cell was evaluated for stability after 200 h, showing a reduction in efficiency from 0.18 % to 0.02 % [4]. WO3 nanoflowers, synthesized by Patil et al., were used as DSSC photoanodes and sensitized with Betanin dye from Beta vulgaris. After 504 h, the efficiency of the device decreased from 3.52 % to 2.86 % [5].

To overcome the challenges associated with low PCE and to improve the stability of DSSCs, researchers have focused on integrating carbon nanostructures into the TiO2 matrix. Carbon nanostructures, including CDs, have been found effective in enhancing the linkage between the TiO2 matrix, leading to faster electron transport [17]. CDs offer additional advantages, such as high photostability, broad absorption spectrum, and excellent solubility in water, making them a promising addition to the TiO2 matrix [18]. The incorporation of ZnO and CDs into the TiO2 matrix appears to be a promising approach for enhancing the performance of DSSCs, particularly with regard to long-term stability and greater energy efficiency [19].

In the present work, ZnO-CDs composites are used as a co-activation material to improve the performance of DSSC. Microwave synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles were used as the photoanode material and Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye and Allium Cepa peel dye were used as photosensitizers to realize the low cost fabrication of DSSC. The co-activation with CDs has led to a significant improvement in PCEs of modified devices compared to unmodified devices. For the CD modified photoanodes the percentage increase in efficiencies are 41.43 % and 48.53 % for Allium Cepa peel dye and Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye, respectively. Whereas ZnO-CD composite integrated photoanodes offered even higher percentage increments of 71.9 % and 66.67 % with Allium Cepa peel dye and Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye, respectively. Additionally, co-activation has improved the stability of the devices. The unmodified photoanodes ceased to function after eight days, while CDs co-activated devices with Hibiscus rosa-sinensis and Allium Cepa peel dye retained 24.92 % and 23.17 % of the initial efficiency, respectively, even after 60 days. For ZnO-CD composite co-activated photoanodes, the percentage of retained efficiencies are 61.50 % and 68.53 % with Allium Cepa peel dye and Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye, respectively. Hence, we believe the obtained ZnO-CDs/TiO2 ternary nanocomposite is an excellent photoanode material to improve the performance of natural DSSC.

TiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized from Titanium isopropoxide (TTIP) (Sisco Research Laboratories, India) using Anton-Parr Monowave 400. ZnO nanoparticles were synthesized by treating Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (S D Fine Chem Limited, India) with NaOH purchased from Merck. Iodolyte HI-30 was obtained from Solaronix SA, Switzerland, while acetonitrile, ethanol, Nitric acid, acetic acid and tert-butyl alcohol were purchased from Merck.

The XRD analysis of the photoanode material was conducted with Rigaku-600 with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The morphology of the synthesized nanoparticles was analyzed with Scanning Electron Microscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific Apreo S LoVac) and a Jeol/JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope. The optical characterizations were performed using Ocean Optics, JAZA 3228 UV–Visible Spectrometer and Horiba Scientific, Duetta fluorescence and absorbance spectrometer. The J-V characteristics of the fabricated devices were studied with Photo Emission Tech Inc. Solar simulator SS50AAA-EM and Keithley source meter.

To prepare TiO2 nanoparticles, an initial mixture of 0.4 mL of nitric acid and 11 mL of distilled water was continuously stirred at 80 °C. Separately, 3.5 mL of TTIP was diluted with 2 mL of acetic acid and then added to the first mixture, maintaining constant stirring. The resulting white precipitate was transferred to a Teflon-sealed glass vial and subjected to microwave treatment at 80 °C for 10 min. The translucent suspension obtained was then dried overnight at 80 °C, yielding TiO2 nanoparticles for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and ZnO-CDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) was accomplished by subjecting Citrus medica fruit extract to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 12 h. The resulting product was purified through centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and vacuum filtration using a 0.22 μm nylon membrane to remove larger particles. Following this, the synthesis of ZnO-CDs was performed using an efficient microwave technique. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (60 mg) was dissolved in 10 mL of the CDs solution and stirred for 30 min. NH3 solution was then added dropwise to the mixture under continuous stirring until the pH reached 7. The mixture was transferred to a sealed glass vial and treated in a monowave reactor at 200 °C for 10 min, resulting in a solution of ZnO-CDs nanocomposite. Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic representation of the synthesis of CDs and the ZnO-CDs nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hibiscus rosa-sinensis and Allium Cepa peel dye extraction
ce:section.ce:para: Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye and Allium cepa peel dye were chosen to sensitize the solar devices, with ethanol/water selected as the extraction solvent. To prepare the Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye, 10 g of Hibiscus rosa-sinensis petals were crushed in a mortar, transferred to a beaker, and soaked in ethanol for 24 h. The mixture was then filtered and heated at 40 °C for 1 h. Similarly, to prepare the Allium cepa peel dye, 10 g of cleaned and dried Allium cepa peels were heated in water at 90 °C for 24 h. The obtained dyes were allowed to cool and were stored in a refrigerator for future use.

The fabrication process of photoanodes with pure TiO2 involves the comminution of 2 g of synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles, 1 mL of acetic acid, and 0.8 mL of Triton X-100 in an agate mortar and pestle for 45 min to yield a uniform colloidal paste. In the same manner, co-activated photoanodes are produced with the incorporation of CDs or ZnO-CDs into the TiO2. The substrate, Indium-doped Tin oxide (ITO), with a resistivity of 10 Ω/sq, is subjected to ultrasonic cleaning with ethanol and deionized water for 30 min and dried before being coated with the photoanode material using the doctor blading method. A uniform paste is coated onto an area of 0.25 cm2 on the ITO substrate. The as-prepared films are gradually annealed to a temperature of 350 °C, followed by a cooling process to 80 °C, and subsequently dipped in Hibiscus rosa-sinensis/Allium Cepa peel dye. After 48 h, the photoanodes are washed with ethanol to eliminate any unbound dye contents.

Counter electrodes are then fabricated by applying Platisol T/sp onto another ITO substrate using the doctor blade technique, followed by sintering at 350 °C for 1 h. Once the electrodes have cooled, the complete fabrication of DSSC is accomplished by sandwiching both electrodes (dye-absorbed photoanode and counter electrode) with binder clips after injecting the Iodolyte HI-30 electrolyte into it. Stability studies are conducted from day 1 to day 60 at 10-day intervals. Three sets of each device are made to limit the error bar and present the best device parameters.

The properties of the DSSCs were assessed through the analysis of their current-voltage (I–V) characteristics using a solar simulator which is designed to replicate solar illumination. This solar simulator operates at a standard light intensity of 1000 W/m2, simulating the AM 1.5G spectrum, which serves as the standard reference spectrum for solar cell testing. To assess the I–V characteristics, the DSSC was connected the Keithley source meter, which is equipped to both apply a voltage across the cell and measure the resulting current. At each incremental voltage, the source meter recorded the corresponding current, resulting in a series of (I–V) data points. Subsequently, these data points were plotted to generate the I–V curve. Additionally, stability studies were conducted over a 60-day duration, with measurements taken at 10-day intervals, commencing from the initial day of fabrication. In order to ensure the reliability of the results, three sets of each device were prepared, thereby minimizing the impact of experimental errors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological analysis of ZnO-CDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a shows the XRD patterns of CDs, TiO2, ZnO, and ZnO-CDs/TiO2. The XRD spectra of CDs exhibited a broad peak at 2θ = 21.90° confirming the amorphous nature of the obtained CDs due to the highly disordered carbon atoms present in it [20]. In the XRD profile corresponding to TiO2, the peaks observed at 25.20o, 37.76o, 47.93o, 53.95o, 55.02o, 62.71o, 68.75o, 70.34o and 75.01o correspond to (101), (004), (200), (105), (211) (204), (116), (220) and (215) planes of anatase TiO2 [21] For ZnO nanoparticles peaks were positioned in the XRD pattern at 31.71°, 34.43°, 36.19°, 47.48°, 56.47°, 62.85°, 66.22°, 67.84°, 68.85°, 72.60°, 76.92° corresponding to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (004) and (202) planes of ZnO [22]. The XRD profile of the ZnO-CDs/TiO2 shows the peaks corresponding to TiO2 and ZnO nanoparticles overlapped with the broad peak of carbon dots which indicates the successful formation of the composite.

The TEM image of the ZnO-CDs/TiO2 composite proves the coexistence of CDs with TiO2 and ZnO nanoparticles (Fig. 2b). The HR-TEM image of the composite with Fourier transform (FFT) images and lattice spacing is shown in Fig. 2c. As shown in images, lattice spacings corresponding to (101) plane of TiO2, (002) plane of CDs, and (101) plane of ZnO were calculated as 0.36 nm, 0.48 nm and 0.26 nm respectively [23–26].

Fig. S1 shows the FTIR spectra of the ZnO-CDs/TiO2 composite. The characteristic peak observed at 436 cm−1 is corresponding to the Zn–O stretching vibrations [27]. Whereas, the peak at 706 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of the Ti–O–O bond [28]. The peaks corresponding to 1118 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibration of C–H and the stretching vibration of CO [29,30]. The broad peak around 3327 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H stretching of carboxylic acid [31]. From the EDS spectra of the ZnO-CDs/TiO2 composite (Fig. S2), the presence of carbon (5.45 Atomic %) oxygen (88.83 Atomic %) Titanium (1.62 Atomic %) and Zinc (5.45 Atomic %) are identified.

In Fig. 3 a and b we can observe the UV–vis absorption spectra and corresponding bandgap values for three different samples: TiO2, CDs/TiO2, and ZnO-CDs/TiO2. The absorbance of TiO2 is mainly in the UV region with maximum absorbance at 273.59 nm. It is clear from the absorption spectra that the absorption edge has extended with strong absorption over a wide range upon the addition of carbon dots. Moreover, the absorbance intensity is further enhanced with the combined action of TiO2 with ZnO-CDs, resulting in the ZnO-CDs/TiO2 composite. In the case of ZnO-CDs/TiO2, the maximum peak was around 330.95 nm with a shoulder peak around 424.39 nm is observed. The TiO2 has a bandgap of 3.6 eV which is decreased to 3.3 eV by the addition of CDs. The presence of ZnO-CDs further decreases the bandgap to 3.2 eV. These results indicate that the ZnO-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite possesses good photovoltaic activity due to its better absorption properties making it a promising material than its counterparts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and stability analysis of fabricated DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The output parameters of all the prepared devices were evaluated using J-V analysis. The devices prepared using bare photoanode (TiO2), carbon dot co-activated photoanode (CDs/TiO2) and ZnO-CDs/TiO2 photoanode sensitized in both dyes are shown in Fig. 4 (a–f). Fig. 4(a–c) plots the working of device in Allium Cepa dye. The device with the unmodified TiO2 had an open circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.59 V, a short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 6.45 mA/cm2, a fill factor (FF) of 52.5 %, and an efficiency of 2.10 %. However after eight days, the devices were shorted and showed no output results. On the other hand, the devices with CDs/TiO2 photoanode sensitized with Allium Cepa dye had a Voc of 0.73 V, Jsc of 6.64 mA/cm2, FF of 61.27 %, and an efficiency of 2.97 %. These devices were stable for more than 60 days, which indicates their superior stability. There is a decrease in Jsc as the days proceed, which indicates that the ability of the photoanode to uphold the dye to produce the carriers has decreased. The co-activation with ZnO-CDs further enhanced all the output parameters namely Voc (0.81 V), Jsc (6.79 mA/cm2) and FF (65.70 %) and the devices remained stable up to 60 days.

Fig. 4(d–f) plots the working of device in Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye. The device with the unmodified TiO2 with Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye showed a Voc of 0.66 V, Jsc of 6 mA/cm2, a FF of 51.60 %, and an efficiency of 2.04 %. The CD co-activated devices sensitized with Hibiscus rosa-sinensis had Voc of 0.72 V, Jsc of 6.54 mA/cm2, FF of 64.4 %, and an efficiency of 3.03 %. With the addition of ZnO nanoparticles, the Voc has been improved to 0.80 V, Jsc to 6.62 mA/cm2, FF to 64.20 %, and efficiency to 3.40 %. The output parameters obtained for each device with both dyes are tabulated and are given in Table S1 and Table S2. The present study highlights the exceptional performance of devices with co-activated photoanodes and demonstrates their suitability for practical applications.

Thus the co-activated photoanodes enhance the stability and reliability of devices. The use of ZnO-CDs/TiO2 photoanodes sensitized with Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye or with Allium Cepa peel dye can lead to significant improvements in device performance and stability making them suitable for practical applications in various fields.

This study demonstrates the effectiveness of a co-activation strategy using microwave-synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles, Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye, and Allium Cepa peel dye for creating low-cost dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The co-activated photoanodes with natural sensitizers significantly improved both efficiency and stability compared to bare photoanodes. The ZnO-CDs modified photoanodes showed the highest improvement, with efficiency increases of 71.90 % (with Allium Cepa peel dye) and 66.67 % (with Hibiscus rosa-sinensis dye) compared to bare photoanodes. Additionally, the co-activated devices demonstrated extended stability, retaining 68.53 % and 61.5 % of their initial efficiencies even after 60 days.

Meera Varghese: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Gisa Grace Ninan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Manoj Balachandran: Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Centre for Research and Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF), CHRIST (Deemed to be University) for their generous financial support under file no. SMSS-2221 and for providing the necessary instrumentation facilities that were instrumental in the successful completion of this research. Furthermore, the authors would like to express their gratitude to DST-FIST for providing the research fund under SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C). The authors would also like to acknowledge the kind support of SAIF-STIC, CUSAT (Kerala) and SAIF-MGU (Kerala) in the characterization studies that were conducted for this research. Their assistance was invaluable in enabling the completion of our study.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.micrna.2024.207934.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanocarbon assisted green hydrogen production: development and recent trends
Int. J. Hydrogen Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Kumar, J. E. Abraham, M. Varghese, J. George, M. Balachandran, and J. Cherusseri, “Nanocarbon assisted green hydrogen production: Development and recent trends,” Int. J. Hydrogen Energy, 2023, doi: 10.1016/j.ijhydene.2023.07.257.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of improved dye-sensitized solar cell for renewable energy power generation
Sol. Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. E. Ikpesu, S. E. Iyuke, M. Daramola, and A. O. Okewale, “Synthesis of improved dye-sensitized solar cell for renewable energy power generation,” Sol. Energy, vol. 206, pp. 918-934, 2020, doi: 10.1016/j.solener.2020.05.002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review on dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs): advanced techniques and research trends
Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Gong, K. Sumathy, Q. Qiao, and Z. Zhou, “Review on dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs): Advanced techniques and research trends,” Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev., vol. 68, pp. 234-246, 2017, doi: 10.1016/j.rser.2016.09.097.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient iron phosphide catalyst as a counter electrode in dye-sensitized solar cells
ACS Appl. Energy Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Yildiz et al., “Efficient iron phosphide catalyst as a counter electrode in dye-sensitized solar cells,” ACS Appl. Energy Mater., vol. 4, no. 10, pp. 10618-10626, 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile fabrication of low‐cost low‐temperature carbon‐based counter electrode with an outstanding fill factor of 73% for dye‐sensitized solar cells
Int. J. Energy Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Altinkaya, A. Atli, A. Atilgan, K. Salimi, and A. Yildiz, “Facile fabrication of low-cost low-temperature carbon-based counter electrode with an outstanding fill factor of 73% for dye-sensitized solar cells,” Int. J. Energy Res., vol. 44, no. 4, pp. 3160-3170, 2020.
